question:
7. For each positive integer \( n \), let \( f_{n} \) be a real-valued symmetric function of \( n \) real variables. Suppose that for all \( n \) and for all real numbers \( x_{1}, \ldots \), \( x_{n+1}, y \), it is true that
(1) \( f_{n}\left(x_{1}+y, \ldots, x_{n}+y\right)=f_{n}\left(x_{1}, \ldots, x_{n}\right)+y \),
(2) \( f_{n}\left(-x_{1}, \ldots,-x_{n}\right)=-f_{n}\left(x_{1}, \ldots x_{n}\right) \),
(3) \( f_{n+1}\left(f_{n}\left(x_{1}, \ldots, x_{n}\right), \ldots, f_{n}\left(x_{1}, \ldots, x_{n}\right), x_{n+1}\right)=f_{n+1}\left(x_{1}, \ldots, x_{n+1}\right) \).

Prove that
(4) \( f_{n}\left(x_{1}, \ldots, x_{n}\right)=\left(x_{1}+\cdots+x_{n}\right) / n \).
solution:
Solution. Since \( f_{1}(0)=-f_{1}(0) \) by (2), we see that
\[
f_{1}(0)=0 .
\]

By (1) and (5) we have
\[
f_{1}(x)=f_{1}(0)+x=x
\]
and this relation is (4) for the case \( n=1 \).
For any integer \( n>1 \) and any number \( c \)
\[
\begin{aligned}
f_{n}(c, 0, \ldots, 0,-c) & =f_{n}(-c, 0, \ldots, 0, c) \\
& =-f_{n}(c, 0, \ldots, 0,-c)
\end{aligned}
\]
by symmetry and by (2); hence
\[
f_{n}(c, 0, \ldots, 0,-c)=0 .
\]

We now make the inductive hypothesis that (4) is valid for \( n=k \). Then equation (3) says that \( f_{k+1}\left(x_{1}, x_{2}, \ldots, x_{k+1}\right) \) depends only on \( x_{k+1} \) and the sum of \( x_{1}, \ldots, x_{k} \). Hence, if \( a_{1}+a_{2}+\cdots+a_{k+1}=0 \), then
\[
f_{k+1}\left(a_{1}, a_{2}, \ldots, a_{k+1}\right)=f_{k+1}\left(-a_{k+1}, 0, \ldots, 0, a_{k+1}\right)=0,
\]
using (6).
Now let \( x_{1}, x_{2}, \ldots, x_{k+1} \) be any numbers and let \( u \) be their average. Set \( a=x_{i}-u \). Then \( a_{1}+a_{2}+\cdots+a_{k, 1}=0 \), and
\[
f_{k+1}\left(x_{1}, x_{2}, \ldots, x_{k+1}\right)=f_{k+1}\left(a_{1}, a_{2}, \ldots, a_{k+1}\right)+u=u
\]
by (1) and (7). This is (4) for \( n=k+1 \). Therefore (4) is true for all integers by induction.

descriptive_long.question:
7. For each positive integer \( countint \), let \( funccount \) be a real-valued symmetric function of \( countint \) real variables. Suppose that for all \( countint \) and for all real numbers \( firstxvar, \ldots \), \( nextxvar, shiftvar \), it is true that
(1) \( funccount\left(firstxvar+shiftvar, \ldots, finalxvar+shiftvar\right)=funccount\left(firstxvar, \ldots, finalxvar\right)+shiftvar \),
(2) \( funccount\left(-firstxvar, \ldots,-finalxvar\right)=-funccount\left(firstxvar, \ldots finalxvar\right) \),
(3) \( funcnextn\left(funccount\left(firstxvar, \ldots, finalxvar\right), \ldots, funccount\left(firstxvar, \ldots, finalxvar\right), nextxvar\right)=funcnextn\left(firstxvar, \ldots, nextxvar\right) \).

Prove that
(4) \( funccount\left(firstxvar, \ldots, finalxvar\right)=\left(firstxvar+\cdots+finalxvar\right) / countint \).
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. Since \( funcfirst(0)=-funcfirst(0) \) by (2), we see that
\[
funcfirst(0)=0 .
\]

By (1) and (5) we have
\[
funcfirst(genericx)=funcfirst(0)+genericx=genericx
\]
and this relation is (4) for the case \( countint=1 \).
For any integer \( countint>1 \) and any number \( constvar \)
\[
\begin{aligned}
funccount(constvar, 0, \ldots, 0,-constvar) & =funccount(-constvar, 0, \ldots, 0, constvar) \\
& =-funccount(constvar, 0, \ldots, 0,-constvar)
\end{aligned}
\]
by symmetry and by (2); hence
\[
funccount(constvar, 0, \ldots, 0,-constvar)=0 .
\]

We now make the inductive hypothesis that (4) is valid for \( countint=stageidx \). Then equation (3) says that \( funcstage\left(firstxvar, x_{2}, \ldots, stepxnext\right) \) depends only on \( stepxnext \) and the sum of \( firstxvar, x_{2}, \ldots, x_{stageidx} \). Hence, if \( firstavar+a_{2}+\cdots+nextavar=0 \), then
\[
funcstage\left(firstavar, a_{2}, \ldots, nextavar\right)=funcstage\left(-nextavar, 0, \ldots, 0, nextavar\right)=0,
\]
using (6).
Now let \( firstxvar, x_{2}, \ldots, stepxnext \) be any numbers and let \( averageu \) be their average. Set \( offseta=x_{i}-averageu \). Then \( firstavar+a_{2}+\cdots+a_{stageidx, 1}=0 \), and
\[
funcstage\left(firstxvar, x_{2}, \ldots, stepxnext\right)=funcstage\left(firstavar, a_{2}, \ldots, nextavar\right)+averageu=averageu
\]
by (1) and (7). This is (4) for \( countint=stageidx+1 \). Therefore (4) is true for all integers by induction.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
7. For each positive integer \( cityscape \), let \( hummingbird \) be a real-valued symmetric function of \( cityscape \) real variables. Suppose that for all \( cityscape \) and for all real numbers \( tangerine, \ldots, honeysuckle, buttercup \), it is true that
(1) \( hummingbird\left(tangerine+buttercup, \ldots, blackbird+buttercup\right)=hummingbird\left(tangerine, \ldots, blackbird\right)+buttercup \),
(2) \( hummingbird\left(-tangerine, \ldots,-blackbird\right)=-hummingbird\left(tangerine, \ldots blackbird\right) \),
(3) \( caterpillar\left(hummingbird\left(tangerine, \ldots, blackbird\right), \ldots, hummingbird\left(tangerine, \ldots, blackbird\right), honeysuckle\right)=caterpillar\left(tangerine, \ldots, honeysuckle\right) \).

Prove that
(4) \( hummingbird\left(tangerine, \ldots, blackbird\right)=\left(tangerine+\cdots+blackbird\right) / cityscape \).
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution. Since \( butterscotch(0)=-butterscotch(0) \) by (2), we see that
\[
butterscotch(0)=0 .
\]

By (1) and (5) we have
\[
butterscotch(marigold)=butterscotch(0)+marigold=marigold
\]
and this relation is (4) for the case \( cityscape=1 \).
For any integer \( cityscape>1 \) and any number \( dragonfly \)
\[
\begin{aligned}
hummingbird(dragonfly, 0, \ldots, 0,-dragonfly) & =hummingbird(-dragonfly, 0, \ldots, 0, dragonfly) \\
& =-hummingbird(dragonfly, 0, \ldots, 0,-dragonfly)
\end{aligned}
\]
by symmetry and by (2); hence
\[
hummingbird(dragonfly, 0, \ldots, 0,-dragonfly)=0 .
\]

We now make the inductive hypothesis that (4) is valid for \( cityscape=woodpecker \). Then equation (3) says that \( quartzstone\left(tangerine, x_{2}, \ldots, pebblestone\right) \) depends only on \( pebblestone \) and the sum of \( tangerine, x_{2}, \ldots, x_{woodpecker} \). Hence, if \( stallion+a_{2}+\cdots+lumberjack=0 \), then
\[
quartzstone\left(stallion, a_{2}, \ldots, lumberjack\right)=quartzstone\left(-lumberjack, 0, \ldots, 0, lumberjack\right)=0,
\]
using (6).
Now let \( tangerine, x_{2}, \ldots, pebblestone \) be any numbers and let \( snowflake \) be their average. Set \( watermelon=x_{i}-snowflake \). Then \( stallion+a_{2}+\cdots+a_{k, 1}=0 \), and
\[
quartzstone\left(tangerine, x_{2}, \ldots, pebblestone\right)=quartzstone\left(stallion, a_{2}, \ldots, lumberjack\right)+snowflake=snowflake
\]
by (1) and (7). This is (4) for \( cityscape=woodpecker+1 \). Therefore (4) is true for all integers by induction.

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
7. For each positive integer \( noninteger \), let \( constantseries \) be a real-valued symmetric function of \( noninteger \) real variables. Suppose that for all \( noninteger \) and for all real numbers \( constantfirst, \ldots \), \( constantfuture, zeroincrement \), it is true that
(1) \( constantseries\left(constantfirst+zeroincrement, \ldots, constantelement+zeroincrement\right)=constantseries\left(constantfirst, \ldots, constantelement\right)+zeroincrement \),
(2) \( constantseries\left(-constantfirst, \ldots,-constantelement\right)=-constantseries\left(constantfirst, \ldots constantelement\right) \),
(3) \( constantupseries\left(constantseries\left(constantfirst, \ldots, constantelement\right), \ldots, constantseries\left(constantfirst, \ldots, constantelement\right), constantfuture\right)=constantupseries\left(constantfirst, \ldots, constantfuture\right) \).

Prove that
(4) \( constantseries\left(constantfirst, \ldots, constantelement\right)=\left(constantfirst+\cdots+constantelement\right) / noninteger \).
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution. Since \( constantunit(0)=-constantunit(0) \) by (2), we see that
\[
constantunit(0)=0 .
\]

By (1) and (5) we have
\[
constantunit(constantvalue)=constantunit(0)+constantvalue=constantvalue
\]
and this relation is (4) for the case \( noninteger=1 \).
For any integer \( noninteger>1 \) and any number \( zeroconstant \)
\[
\begin{aligned}
constantseries(zeroconstant, 0, \ldots, 0,-zeroconstant) & =constantseries(-zeroconstant, 0, \ldots, 0, zeroconstant) \\
& =-constantseries(zeroconstant, 0, \ldots, 0,-zeroconstant)
\end{aligned}
\]
by symmetry and by (2); hence
\[
constantseries(zeroconstant, 0, \ldots, 0,-zeroconstant)=0 .
\]

We now make the inductive hypothesis that (4) is valid for \( noninteger=continuum \). Then equation (3) says that \( constantlaterseries\left(constantfirst, x_{2}, \ldots, constantlater\right) \) depends only on \( constantlater \) and the sum of \( constantfirst, x_{2}, \ldots, x_{continuum} \). Hence, if \( fixedfirst+a_{2}+\cdots+fixedlater=0 \), then
\[
constantlaterseries\left(fixedfirst, a_{2}, \ldots, fixedlater\right)=constantlaterseries\left(-fixedlater, 0, \ldots, 0, fixedlater\right)=0,
\]
using (6).
Now let \( constantfirst, x_{2}, \ldots, constantlater \) be any numbers and let \( atypical \) be their average. Set \( fixedscalar=x_{i}-atypical \). Then \( fixedfirst+a_{2}+\cdots+a_{k, 1}=0 \), and
\[
constantlaterseries\left(constantfirst, x_{2}, \ldots, constantlater\right)=constantlaterseries\left(fixedfirst, a_{2}, \ldots, fixedlater\right)+atypical=atypical
\]
by (1) and (7). This is (4) for \( noninteger=continuum+1 \). Therefore (4) is true for all integers by induction.

garbled_string.question:
7. For each positive integer \( rvtmclae \), let \( qrfbgmsa \) be a real-valued symmetric function of \( rvtmclae \) real variables. Suppose that for all \( rvtmclae \) and for all real numbers \( hjgrksla, \ldots \), \( klmtzshq, gdlpxezo \), it is true that
(1) \( qrfbgmsa\left(hjgrksla+gdlpxezo, \ldots, bvfqiode+gdlpxezo\right)=qrfbgmsa\left(hjgrksla, \ldots, bvfqiode\right)+gdlpxezo \),
(2) \( qrfbgmsa\left(-hjgrksla, \ldots,-bvfqiode\right)=-qrfbgmsa\left(hjgrksla, \ldots bvfqiode\right) \),
(3) \( ypxhwzce\left(qrfbgmsa\left(hjgrksla, \ldots, bvfqiode\right), \ldots, qrfbgmsa\left(hjgrksla, \ldots, bvfqiode\right), klmtzshq\right)=ypxhwzce\left(hjgrksla, \ldots, klmtzshq\right) \).

Prove that
(4) \( qrfbgmsa\left(hjgrksla, \ldots, bvfqiode\right)=\left(hjgrksla+\cdots+bvfqiode\right) / rvtmclae \).
garbled_string.solution:
Solution. Since \( hudjwcke(0)=-hudjwcke(0) \) by (2), we see that
\[
hudjwcke(0)=0 .
\]

By (1) and (5) we have
\[
hudjwcke(qzxwvtnp)=hudjwcke(0)+qzxwvtnp=qzxwvtnp
\]
and this relation is (4) for the case \( rvtmclae=1 \).
For any integer \( rvtmclae>1 \) and any number \( znmqwejr \)
\[
\begin{aligned}
qrfbgmsa(znmqwejr, 0, \ldots, 0,-znmqwejr) & =qrfbgmsa(-znmqwejr, 0, \ldots, 0, znmqwejr) \\
& =-qrfbgmsa(znmqwejr, 0, \ldots, 0,-znmqwejr)
\end{aligned}
\]
by symmetry and by (2); hence
\[
qrfbgmsa(znmqwejr, 0, \ldots, 0,-znmqwejr)=0 .
\]

We now make the inductive hypothesis that (4) is valid for \( rvtmclae=sgqdnwop \). Then equation (3) says that \( aogxlemn\left(hjgrksla, x_{2}, \ldots, wyfrsnpd\right) \) depends only on \( wyfrsnpd \) and the sum of \( hjgrksla, \ldots, x_{k} \). Hence, if \( lomnzska+a_{2}+\cdots+dvrhcgta=0 \), then
\[
aogxlemn\left(lomnzska, a_{2}, \ldots, dvrhcgta\right)=aogxlemn\left(-dvrhcgta, 0, \ldots, 0, dvrhcgta\right)=0,
\]
using (6).
Now let \( hjgrksla, x_{2}, \ldots, wyfrsnpd \) be any numbers and let \( kdsplmno \) be their average. Set \( xpqjvhrt=x_{i}-kdsplmno \). Then \( lomnzska+a_{2}+\cdots+dvrhcgta=0 \), and
\[
aogxlemn\left(hjgrksla, x_{2}, \ldots, wyfrsnpd\right)=aogxlemn\left(lomnzska, a_{2}, \ldots, dvrhcgta\right)+kdsplmno=kdsplmno
\]
by (1) and (7). This is (4) for \( rvtmclae=sgqdnwop+1 \). Therefore (4) is true for all integers by induction.

kernel_variant.question:
Let n be a positive integer and let F_n be a real-valued function of n real variables that is symmetric in its arguments.  Assume that for every n \geq  1, for all real numbers x_1 , \ldots  , x_{n+1} and for every real number y the following three properties hold:

(A)  Translation invariance
   F_n(x_1+y , \ldots  , x_n+y) = F_n(x_1 , \ldots  , x_n)+y.

(B)  Oddness
   F_n(-x_1 , \ldots  , -x_n) = -F_n(x_1 , \ldots  , x_n).

(C)  Self-reproduction
   F_{n+1}(x_{n+1} , F_n(x_1 , \ldots  , x_n) , \ldots  , F_n(x_1 , \ldots  , x_n)) = F_{n+1}(x_1 , \ldots  , x_{n+1}),
   where on the left the value F_n(x_1 , \ldots  , x_n) appears exactly n times.

Prove that for every n \geq  1 and every real x_1 , \ldots  , x_n,
            F_n(x_1 , \ldots  , x_n) = (x_1+\cdots +x_n)/n .
kernel_variant.solution:
We prove by induction on n that
        F_n(x_1 , \ldots  , x_n) = (x_1+\cdots +x_n)/n    (*)
for all real x_1 , \ldots  , x_n.

Base case  n = 1.
Property (B) with n = 1 gives F_1(0)=-F_1(0), hence F_1(0)=0.  Then (A) yields for any real x
        F_1(x)=F_1(0+x)=F_1(0)+x=x,
so (*) holds when n = 1.

Inductive step.
Assume (*) is true for some fixed k \geq  1; we show it for k+1.

1.  A convenient zero value.
Because F_{k+1} is symmetric, for every c \in  \mathbb{R}
        F_{k+1}(c,0,\ldots ,0,-c)=F_{k+1}(-c,0,\ldots ,0,c).
Applying (B) to the right-hand side gives
        F_{k+1}(-c,0,\ldots ,0,c)=-F_{k+1}(c,0,\ldots ,0,-c),
so
        F_{k+1}(c,0,\ldots ,0,-c)=0.            (1)

2.  Dependence on the sum S = x_1+\cdots +x_k.
By (C) and the induction hypothesis
        F_k(x_1,\ldots ,x_k) = S/k.
Therefore
        F_{k+1}(x_1,\ldots ,x_{k+1})
        = F_{k+1}(x_{k+1},S/k,\ldots ,S/k).
Hence x_1,\ldots ,x_k affect the value of F_{k+1} only through the single parameter S.  Consequently there exists a real function G such that
        F_{k+1}(x_1,\ldots ,x_{k+1}) = G(S , x_{k+1}).          (2)
(Note that we do not claim any symmetry of G; only its very existence is needed.)

3.  Vanishing on (k+1)-tuples with zero sum.
Suppose a_1+\cdots +a_{k+1}=0.  Then S = a_1+\cdots +a_k = -a_{k+1}.  Substituting into (2) gives
        F_{k+1}(a_1,\ldots ,a_{k+1}) = G(-a_{k+1}, a_{k+1}).
Putting c = a_{k+1} in (1) we know F_{k+1}(c,0,\ldots ,0,-c)=0; with (2) that value equals G(-c,c).  Hence G(-c,c)=0 for every c, and therefore
        F_{k+1}(a_1,\ldots ,a_{k+1}) = 0 whenever a_1+\cdots +a_{k+1}=0.      (3)

4.  General arguments.
For arbitrary x_1,\ldots ,x_{k+1} set
        m = (x_1+\cdots +x_{k+1})/(k+1),      a_i = x_i - m  (1 \leq  i \leq  k+1).
Then \Sigma  a_i = 0, so by (3)  F_{k+1}(a_1,\ldots ,a_{k+1}) = 0.  Applying (A) with y = m,
        F_{k+1}(x_1,\ldots ,x_{k+1})
        = F_{k+1}(a_1+m,\ldots ,a_{k+1}+m)
        = F_{k+1}(a_1,\ldots ,a_{k+1}) + m   (by (A))
        = 0 + m = (x_1+\cdots +x_{k+1})/(k+1).
Thus (*) holds for n = k+1, completing the induction.

Therefore formula (*) is valid for every positive integer n.

core_steps: Base case n=1: oddness (2) forces f₁(0)=0, then translation rule (1) gives f₁(x)=x
Using symmetry + oddness, show fₙ(c,0,…,0,−c)=0 for any c (zero-sum special vector)
Inductive hypothesis: assume average formula for n=k
Plug hypothesis into recursion (3) ⇒ f_{k+1} depends only on x_{k+1} and Σx₁…x_k; combine with step-2 to get f_{k+1}=0 on every zero-sum (k+1)-tuple
Apply translation rule (1): subtract the mean to reach a zero-sum tuple, then add the mean back, yielding f_{k+1}(x₁,…,x_{k+1}) = (x₁+…+x_{k+1})/(k+1)

slot1.description: Choice of the non-zero entry in the special zero-sum vector used in step-2

slot2.description: Positions of +c and −c inside the n-tuple (they were first and last); any two positions work because the function is symmetric
